metadata_frame: The following passage comes from The History of Canada Under French Régime. 1535-1763. With Maps, Plans, and Illustrative Notes, a history published in 1881 by Henry H. Miles, a Canadian professor.
main_question:
M. de Tonti, a former coadjutor of La Salle, was sent to Michillimakinac, to bring back the French who had taken up their quarters there, and who amounted to more than one hundred persons. A missionary and M. de Courtmanche were employed in soliciting the chiefs of the western tribes to cease from warfare, and to bring all their Iroquois prisoners to the proposed meeting at Montreal. In the meantime, the agents of the New England colonies were engaged in endeavours to counteract the influence of the French among the cantons, [masked clause revising an implied expectation]. A severe law against Jesuit priests entering the New England territory was declared to be valid in the cantons as well as other parts.

Write a clause appropriate for this book that could stand in the position marked by [masked clause revising an implied expectation]:
source_title: The History of Canada Under French Régime. 1535-1763. With Maps, Plans, and Illustrative Notes
answer_strings: but with no great success
but with considerable success
but the displaced councillors were restored to their former positions, and suitable steps taken in regard to the liquor traffic, tithes, and other occasions of discord
to prevent them from converting more Indians
to suppress French missionary influence
passage: M. de Tonti, a former coadjutor of La Salle, was sent to Michillimakinac, to bring back the French who had taken up their quarters there, and who amounted to more than one hundred persons. A missionary and M. de Courtmanche were employed in soliciting the chiefs of the western tribes to cease from warfare, and to bring all their Iroquois prisoners to the proposed meeting at Montreal. In the meantime, the agents of the New England colonies were engaged in endeavours to counteract the influence of the French among the cantons, but with no great success. A severe law against Jesuit priests entering the New England territory was declared to be valid in the cantons as well as other parts.

metadata_frame: The following passage comes from The History of Canada Under French Régime. 1535-1763. With Maps, Plans, and Illustrative Notes, a history published in 1881 by Henry H. Miles, a Canadian professor.
main_question:
These had even promised to migrate in a body, but when the period arrived for fulfilling the promise they refused; and, not feeling safe on the island, applied to the Governor for protection. This D'Aillebout accorded by allowing the remnant of their number to move to Quebec and establish their cabins in the vicinity of Fort St Louis. In the course of 1658, several Frenchmen were killed by Iroquois, [masked clause acknowledging a countervailing fact]. D'Aillebout, in consequence, issued orders to capture all Iroquois who should present themselves about any of the French posts, and that the prisoners should be held as hostages, and as means of exacting penalties for the murders which had been committed.

Write a clause appropriate for this book that could stand in the position marked by [masked clause acknowledging a countervailing fact]:
source_title: The History of Canada Under French Régime. 1535-1763. With Maps, Plans, and Illustrative Notes
answer_strings: although a truce existed at the time
despite ongoing hostilities
but the Iroquois continued to raid French settlements with impunity
although some Iroquois had likewise suffered losses at the hands of French forces
passage: These had even promised to migrate in a body, but when the period arrived for fulfilling the promise they refused; and, not feeling safe on the island, applied to the Governor for protection. This D'Aillebout accorded by allowing the remnant of their number to move to Quebec and establish their cabins in the vicinity of Fort St Louis. In the course of 1658, several Frenchmen were killed by Iroquois, although a truce existed at the time. D'Aillebout, in consequence, issued orders to capture all Iroquois who should present themselves about any of the French posts, and that the prisoners should be held as hostages, and as means of exacting penalties for the murders which had been committed.

metadata_frame: The following passage comes from The History of Canada Under French Régime. 1535-1763. With Maps, Plans, and Illustrative Notes, a history published in 1881 by Henry H. Miles, a Canadian professor.
main_question:
Additions were also made to the defences of Montreal and Quebec. In the latter city new redoubts were constructed, and the means of defence further improved by the completion of gateways surmounted by battlements at the two western points of exit. The inhabitants on the lands above and below Quebec were directed, in case of an invasion, to remove their families and property, [masked clause describing a cause or reason]. All these precautions and preparations turned out to be unnecessary.

Write a clause appropriate for this book that could stand in the position marked by [masked clause describing a cause or reason]:
source_title: The History of Canada Under French Régime. 1535-1763. With Maps, Plans, and Illustrative Notes
answer_strings: for concealment, into the recesses of the neighbouring forests, and then to dispatch their able-bodied men to the defence of the city
for exposure, out from the confines of the surrounding forests, and then to send their elderly and infirm to abandon the city
in order to establish themselves and erect batteries in positions convenient for bombarding the city

because, according to Knox, they had been too "roughly handled" in the fight, General Murray ordered the gates to be closed, and the guards on the ramparts, who had not been relieved since morning, to be changed
in order to prevent them from falling into the hands of the enemy
so that the enemy would be deprived of local resources and shelter
passage: Additions were also made to the defences of Montreal and Quebec. In the latter city new redoubts were constructed, and the means of defence further improved by the completion of gateways surmounted by battlements at the two western points of exit. The inhabitants on the lands above and below Quebec were directed, in case of an invasion, to remove their families and property, for concealment, into the recesses of the neighbouring forests, and then to dispatch their able-bodied men to the defence of the city. All these precautions and preparations turned out to be unnecessary.

metadata_frame: The following passage comes from The History of Canada Under French Régime. 1535-1763. With Maps, Plans, and Illustrative Notes, a history published in 1881 by Henry H. Miles, a Canadian professor.
main_question:
The campaign can scarcely last another month, in consequence of the autumnal gales, which are here so disastrous to shipping, and so severe. It might seem that after so favourable a prelude, the safety of the colony can scarcely be doubtful. [masked sentence revising an implied expectation]

Write a sentence appropriate for this book that could stand in the position marked by [masked sentence revising an implied expectation]:
source_title: The History of Canada Under French Régime. 1535-1763. With Maps, Plans, and Illustrative Notes
answer_strings: Such, however, is not the case, as the capture of Quebec depends on a coup-de-main.
Such, however, is the case, as the capture of Quebec does not depend on a coup-de-main.
But, as an offset to the general conditions which favoured the welfare of the colony, the inhabitants, both French and Indians, were troubled with maladies which proved fatal to many.
However, one must remain cautious as the future holds many uncertainties.
Yet the looming winter, with its relentless squalls and the colony’s dwindling stores, threatened to undo the optimism.
passage: The campaign can scarcely last another month, in consequence of the autumnal gales, which are here so disastrous to shipping, and so severe. It might seem that after so favourable a prelude, the safety of the colony can scarcely be doubtful. Such, however, is not the case, as the capture of Quebec depends on a coup-de-main.

metadata_frame: The following passage comes from The History of Canada Under French Régime. 1535-1763. With Maps, Plans, and Illustrative Notes, a history published in 1881 by Henry H. Miles, a Canadian professor.
main_question:
repressed only by means of the gallows and other extreme modes of punishment; disease, which carried off a quarter of his followers in the course of the ensuing winter; unsuccessful attempts at exploration, attended with considerable loss of life; and finally, famine, which reduced the surviving French to a state of abject dependence upon the natives for the salvation of their lives. Roberval had sent one of his vessels back to France, with urgent demands for succour; but the King, instead of acceding to his petition, dispatched orders for him to return home. It is stated, on somewhat doubtful authority, that Cartier himself was deputed to bring home the relics of the expedition; and, [masked clause describing a condition or proviso], this distinguished navigator must have made a fourth voyage out to the regions which he had been the first to make known to the world. Thus ended Roberval's abortive attempt to establish a French colony on the banks of the St Lawrence.

Write a clause appropriate for this book that could stand in the position marked by [masked clause describing a condition or proviso]:
source_title: The History of Canada Under French Régime. 1535-1763. With Maps, Plans, and Illustrative Notes
answer_strings: if so
if not
if
unless the Government of France could be made to recognise the necessity of forwarding aid sufficient to deliver the colonists from ruin
unless he had already left to return home
provided that the reports were accurate
passage: repressed only by means of the gallows and other extreme modes of punishment; disease, which carried off a quarter of his followers in the course of the ensuing winter; unsuccessful attempts at exploration, attended with considerable loss of life; and finally, famine, which reduced the surviving French to a state of abject dependence upon the natives for the salvation of their lives. Roberval had sent one of his vessels back to France, with urgent demands for succour; but the King, instead of acceding to his petition, dispatched orders for him to return home. It is stated, on somewhat doubtful authority, that Cartier himself was deputed to bring home the relics of the expedition; and, if so, this distinguished navigator must have made a fourth voyage out to the regions which he had been the first to make known to the world. Thus ended Roberval's abortive attempt to establish a French colony on the banks of the St Lawrence.

metadata_frame: The following passage comes from The History of Canada Under French Régime. 1535-1763. With Maps, Plans, and Illustrative Notes, a history published in 1881 by Henry H. Miles, a Canadian professor.
main_question:
The preliminaries having been arranged, happily without the occurrence of quarrels so likely to take place in such a concourse of individuals belonging to different nations, the ceremonies and customary distribution of presents were followed by a mutual interchange of stipulations, rendered intelligible to all by means of interpreters. The final result was a treaty of peace, to which the chief contracting parties were the French, the Hurons, the Algonquins, and the Iroquois, who agreed, thenceforward, to remain on peaceable terms with each other. [masked sentence describing an inference or effect] The 56.

Write a sentence appropriate for this book that could stand in the position marked by [masked sentence describing an inference or effect]:
source_title: The History of Canada Under French Régime. 1535-1763. With Maps, Plans, and Illustrative Notes
answer_strings: The peace thus established was not of long duration.
The peace thus established was of long duration.

Thus the operations against Fort Niagara were entirely successful, involving in the result the easy capture of the other French forts by General Stanwix, since their garrisons had been reduced by the numbers detached for the relief of Captain Pouchot.† 

271.
This treaty significantly reduced tensions among the indigenous tribes and their French allies.
Consequently, the neighboring peoples could resume commerce and cultural exchanges without the fear of sudden conflict.
passage: The preliminaries having been arranged, happily without the occurrence of quarrels so likely to take place in such a concourse of individuals belonging to different nations, the ceremonies and customary distribution of presents were followed by a mutual interchange of stipulations, rendered intelligible to all by means of interpreters. The final result was a treaty of peace, to which the chief contracting parties were the French, the Hurons, the Algonquins, and the Iroquois, who agreed, thenceforward, to remain on peaceable terms with each other. The peace thus established was not of long duration. The 56.

metadata_frame: The following passage comes from The History of Canada Under French Régime. 1535-1763. With Maps, Plans, and Illustrative Notes, a history published in 1881 by Henry H. Miles, a Canadian professor.
main_question:
He was a man of noble and virtuous disposition, chivalrous, and inspired with a deep sense of religion, and at that time about thirty-six years of age. It will also be seen that Champlain was gifted with qualities which endeared him both to his own followers and to the native Indians of Canada. He was of good address-always able, when he desired it, to render himself acceptable to the highest personages in France, [masked clause describing an inference or effect]. Such was the man who, under the auspices of De Chates and of M. de Monts, first made his appearance in New France, in whose early annals he figured conspicuously upwards of thirty years.

Write a clause appropriate for this book that could stand in the position marked by [masked clause describing an inference or effect]:
source_title: The History of Canada Under French Régime. 1535-1763. With Maps, Plans, and Illustrative Notes
answer_strings: so as to secure a willing attention to his representations
so as to escape a willing attention his representations
so long as Champlain lived
so far as these were dependent upon the care of the Associates, their chief officers were left to contend with difficulties similar to those which had formerly beset Champlain
this helped him secure support and resources for his expeditions
and consequently won the confidence and patronage of the highest French authorities
passage: He was a man of noble and virtuous disposition, chivalrous, and inspired with a deep sense of religion, and at that time about thirty-six years of age. It will also be seen that Champlain was gifted with qualities which endeared him both to his own followers and to the native Indians of Canada. He was of good address-always able, when he desired it, to render himself acceptable to the highest personages in France, so as to secure a willing attention to his representations. Such was the man who, under the auspices of De Chates and of M. de Monts, first made his appearance in New France, in whose early annals he figured conspicuously upwards of thirty years.

metadata_frame: The following passage comes from The History of Canada Under French Régime. 1535-1763. With Maps, Plans, and Illustrative Notes, a history published in 1881 by Henry H. Miles, a Canadian professor.
main_question:
It was also about this time that the Iroquois began to establish, on a considerable scale, a regular traffic with the northern and western Indians, in such manner as to draw off the trade in skins from the route of the Lower St Lawrence, and so as to make themselves the channel of communication with the traders of the English colonies, who paid higher prices than the French. De Courcelle, foreseeing that Canadian commerce with the western tribes would be ruined in that way, undertook the expedition to the north shore of Ontario in order to menace the Senecas, as has been already described. [masked sentence describing a cause or reason]

Write a sentence appropriate for this book that could stand in the position marked by [masked sentence describing a cause or reason]:
source_title: The History of Canada Under French Régime. 1535-1763. With Maps, Plans, and Illustrative Notes
answer_strings: The same cause led that Governor to project the establishment of a fort and military station at Cataracoui (Kingston), which was afterwards built by his successor, and called Fort Frontenac.
The same cause led that Governor to abandon the idea of establishing a fort and military station at Cataracoui (Kingston), which was afterwards built by his predecessor, and called Fort Frontenac.

For this purpose he selected the celebrated Nicolas Perrot, and M. Louvigny, an officer of merit, to proceed by the route of the Ottawa river to Michilimakinac with a large convoy, and to carry presents and various supplies of merchandise, such as the savages required for warfare and the chase, as well as for domestic use.
For this purpose he employed an intrepid officer, named Varrenne de la Varendrye, who occupied about fifteen years in conducting those explorations.
The French, recognizing the potential loss of revenue and influence due to English competition, wanted to secure their trade monopoly.
Because the English, offering higher prices, were drawing the fur trade away from French hands, De Courcelle felt compelled to act.
passage: It was also about this time that the Iroquois began to establish, on a considerable scale, a regular traffic with the northern and western Indians, in such manner as to draw off the trade in skins from the route of the Lower St Lawrence, and so as to make themselves the channel of communication with the traders of the English colonies, who paid higher prices than the French. De Courcelle, foreseeing that Canadian commerce with the western tribes would be ruined in that way, undertook the expedition to the north shore of Ontario in order to menace the Senecas, as has been already described. The same cause led that Governor to project the establishment of a fort and military station at Cataracoui (Kingston), which was afterwards built by his successor, and called Fort Frontenac.
